story:
Bobby knew his cell by numbers. Thirteen feet six inches long, nine feet wide, nine feet nine inches high. One hundred twenty-one and a half square feet. Three hundred ninety cinder blocks. He could count them with his eyes closed, pace the gray floor in his sleep. The cold steel door, the slot for his meals, the barred window that offered light but no view—he knew them like a blind man knows a room. The steel desk with his Bible. The stainless toilet-sink that dripped all night. The thin plastic mattress that cracked and groaned. He felt like one more fixture bolted to the place.

Lying on the bunk, he remembered. He measured time in loss. The last time he held Janie: three years, two months, one week, one day. The last time he spoke to her: two years, six months. The last letter from her: more than a year. Then the note from Janie's kid sister, each word burned into him: Janie had gone to Las Vegas with his friend Tommy Spencer. After that, only his mother's letters came.

He hadn't seen his mother since the sentencing. The prison was too far. He didn't want her to look at him through glass, wrapped in orange. The old timers said visits made time harder, especially on family. It was his crime; he would do the time. Then he could go home, home to—

That evening, something strange happened. Bobby felt a pull, a need to be somewhere else. He found himself walking toward the visiting room, though no guard had called him, though the hour was wrong. The room was nearly dark, empty, the glass gleaming between stools. Then he saw the face on the other side, taking a moment to arrange itself into the woman he knew.

"Mom?"

Her voice trembled, soft and sure. She said she'd missed him. He said the same. He asked what she was doing there.

She smiled, the old forgiving smile, the one that had smoothed over every scrape when he was a boy. Light seemed to gather in her eyes. She told him she had come to take him home. She couldn't stay, but tomorrow he would be free, and they would be together. She told him she loved him, pressed her palm to the glass, and blew a kiss.

He told her he loved her, too. Then she was gone. He reached out and touched the spot where her hand had been. The glass was warm.

Back in his cell, something lifted in him. He didn't understand it. He didn't need to. He was going home. Sleep came down easy and complete.

Early the next morning, the warden called for Carson. A guard appeared in the doorway a few minutes later, hat in hand. Carson was dead, he said. It looked peaceful. The doctor was on the way.

The warden stared at the yellow slip on his desk. A phone message. Bobby's mother had passed away at her home three nights earlier.

Bobby had kept count of blocks and days. In the end, a different measure carried him past the steel and the glass. His mother had come, just as she promised, to take him home.
